research_answer: Yes, and the difference is meaningful. High-tech products like electronics, machinery, and medical devices show the strongest predictability under implied comparative advantage frameworks, with R² values of around 0.19–0.20 for growth predictions. By contrast, extractive industries and agricultural products show the weakest predictability, with R² values in the range of 0.14–0.15. The intuition is that high-tech manufacturing requires dense, overlapping capabilities — the same knowledge that makes a country good at one type of machine makes it likely to be good at adjacent machines — while comparative advantage in commodities often depends on geographic luck (e.g., oilfield discoveries or soil conditions) that has little to do with a country's broader technological capabilities. Apparel and textiles also show relatively low predictability (R²≈0.14), likely because labor cost dynamics can shift these industries across countries in ways unrelated to capability relatedness.
supporting_quotes: For product subsamples (based on the HS chapters)... it appears that the most easily-explained categories are high-tech goods like electronics or medical devices. The worst predictions are in the extractives and agricultural categories; this makes sense, since shifts in these commodities may have more to do with geographic luck (e.g., oilfield discoveries) than shared technological requirements.
